cuisine: Global / Multi-regional (Mediterranean, South Asian, Middle Eastern)
region_of_origin: Mediterranean region; widely cultivated across South Asia and the Middle East

allergens: Apiaceae family (potential cross-reactivity in celery-mugwort-spice syndrome)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of fennel seeds is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2g), used as a spice in cooking or consumed as a mouth freshener after meals.

nutrient_source.title: Fennel seeds, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw fennel seeds (F10060024). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices and Seeds — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for whole dried seeds and spices with high dietary fibre content
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fennel seeds do not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on: (1) very high dietary fibre content (39.8g/100g) which substantially attenuates glucose absorption; (2) analogy with other high-fibre whole dried seeds and spices in the University of Sydney GI Database which consistently fall in the low GI range (15–35); (3) typical culinary serving size of 2g renders glycemic load negligible at 0.26 per serving. GL calculated as: GI (25) × available carbohydrate per serving (12.49g net carb × 0.02 serving fraction = 0.2498g) / 100 = 0.26.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (88mg/100g), exceptionally high potassium (1694mg/100g) and calcium (1196mg/100g) supporting vasodilation per DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Fennel seeds are suitable for hypertensive individuals; their high potassium-to-sodium ratio (19:1) actively supports blood pressure reduction, and typical culinary use (1–2g per serving) contributes negligible sodium while providing meaningful potassium and calcium over cumulative dietary intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high dietary fibre (39.8g/100g) slows glucose absorption; estimated low GI (~25); negligible glycemic load per typical serving (GL ~0.26)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fennel seeds are safe and beneficial for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used as a culinary spice; the very high fibre content attenuates postprandial glucose response, and the negligible glycemic load per serving (approximately 0.26) poses no clinically meaningful glycemic burden, though therapeutic doses or fennel seed supplements should be used with blood glucose monitoring due to potential hypoglycaemic effects.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.48g/100g), high dietary fibre (39.8g/100g), high potassium (1694mg/100g), and anti-inflammatory polyphenols (anethole, quercetin, kaempferol)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fennel seeds are cardioprotective when used as a culinary spice; their negligible saturated fat, high soluble fibre supporting LDL reduction, high potassium supporting vascular health, and documented anti-inflammatory phytochemicals collectively contribute to a favourable cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (anethole, quercetin, kaempferol) and high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome modulation of immune response
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fennel seeds may be consumed freely as a culinary spice by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; their polyphenol content provides anti-inflammatory benefit, though those with Apiaceae family allergies should exercise caution due to potential cross-reactivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fennel seeds (Foeniculum vulgare) are small, oval, green-brown dried seeds with a firm texture and a characteristic sweet, anise-like flavour derived primarily from the volatile compound anethole. They are used as a culinary spice across Mediterranean, South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Southeast Asian cuisines — appearing in spice blends such as panch phoron and Chinese five-spice, as well as in Indian curries, sausages, breads, and as a traditional mouth freshener (mukhwas) consumed after meals in South Asia. Nutritionally, fennel seeds are exceptionally dense: per 100g they provide 345 kcal, 15.8g protein, 14.87g fat (predominantly monounsaturated at ~9.91g), 52.29g carbohydrate, and a remarkable 39.8g dietary fibre — one of the highest fibre densities among common spices. They are also outstanding sources of calcium (1196mg/100g), potassium (1694mg/100g), and iron (18.54mg/100g). Sodium is low at 88mg/100g. Clinically, fennel seeds are suitable for individuals managing hypertension (high potassium-to-sodium ratio), Type 2 Diabetes (very high fibre, low estimated GI ~25, negligible glycemic load per serving), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, high fibre, anti-inflammatory polyphenols). They are plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Typical culinary serving is 1–2g (approximately 1 teaspoon), making the per-serving nutritional contribution modest but cumulative across regular use. Known in Hindi as saunf (सौंफ), in Tamil as perunjeeragam (பெருஞ்சீரகம்), in Malay as biji jintan manis, and in Chinese as 茴香籽.
common_user_queries: What are fennel seeds good for?
Are fennel seeds good for diabetes?
Is saunf the same as fennel seeds?
How many calories in fennel seeds?
Are fennel seeds high in fibre?
Can I eat fennel seeds if I have hypertension?
meal_context: ingredient in spice blends (panch phoron, five-spice)

ingredient_synonyms.saunf: Hindi name for fennel seeds, widely used in South Asian cooking and as a post-meal digestive
ingredient_synonyms.sonf: alternate romanization of the Hindi name saunf
ingredient_synonyms.biji jintan manis: Malay name for fennel seeds; jintan manis means sweet cumin
ingredient_synonyms.茴香籽: Chinese (simplified and traditional) name for fennel seeds
ingredient_synonyms.anethole: primary volatile compound responsible for the sweet anise-like flavour of fennel seeds
ingredient_synonyms.Foeniculum vulgare: botanical name for the fennel plant whose seeds are used as a spice

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F10060024) with confidence 0.95. Supplementary micronutrient values (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamins, minerals) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fennel seeds (NDB 02018). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to high-fibre dried seeds. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT public databases.
notes: 1. Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fennel seeds (NDB 02018), which reports total sugars as 0.0g/100g; this is consistent with the seed's carbohydrate profile being dominated by dietary fibre and complex polysaccharides. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 2. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as fennel seeds are a plant-based food; confirmed zero per HPB source. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as these are plant-derived seeds with no animal-derived components; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero; plant-derived seed with no industrial hydrogenation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 5. Saturated fat (0.48g/100g), monounsaturated fat (9.91g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.69g/100g), and all vitamin/mineral values not present in the HPB source are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02018, Spices, fennel seed) as a secondary approved source. 6. GI value of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct published GI for fennel seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. The estimate is conservative and defensible given the 39.8g/100g dietary fibre content. 7. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as fennel seeds are seeds/spices, not whole grains. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC and FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 are cited as defensible matches; reviewers should verify against current database versions. SNOMED CT code 227318003 verified as 'Fennel seed (substance)'.
